**LaMia Flight 2933** was a [charter flight](/wiki/Air_charter "Air charter") of an [Avro RJ85](/wiki/Avro_RJ85 "Avro RJ85"), operated by [LaMia](/wiki/LaMia "LaMia"), that on 28 November 2016 crashed near [Medellín](/wiki/Medell%C3%ADn "Medellín"), Colombia, killing 71 of the 77 people on board. The aircraft was transporting the first-team squad of Brazilian football club [Chapecoense](/wiki/Associa%C3%A7%C3%A3o_Chapecoense_de_Futebol "Associação Chapecoense de Futebol") and their entourage from [Santa Cruz de la Sierra](/wiki/Santa_Cruz_de_la_Sierra "Santa Cruz de la Sierra"), Bolivia, to Medellín, where the team was scheduled to play at the [2016 Copa Sudamericana Finals](/wiki/2016_Copa_Sudamericana_finals "2016 Copa Sudamericana finals"). One of the four crew members, three of the players, and two other passengers survived with injuries.

The official report from Colombia's civil aviation agency, [Aerocivil](/wiki/Colombian_Civil_Aviation_Authority "Colombian Civil Aviation Authority"), found the causes of the crash to be [fuel exhaustion](/wiki/Fuel_starvation "Fuel starvation") due to an inappropriate flight plan by the airline, and [pilot error](/wiki/Pilot_error "Pilot error") regarding poor decision-making as the situation worsened, including a failure to [declare an emergency](/wiki/Mayday "Mayday") for 36 minutes after fuel levels became critically low, thus failing to inform [air traffic control](/wiki/Air_traffic_control "Air traffic control") at Medellín – until just seconds before its [fuel-starved](/wiki/Fuel_starvation "Fuel starvation") engines [flamed out](/wiki/Flameout "Flameout"), and 18 kilometres (9.7 nmi; 11 mi) from the airport – that an immediate landing was required.

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=LaMia_Flight_2933&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background")]

### Aircraft and operator

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=LaMia_Flight_2933&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Aircraft and operator")]

The aircraft was an [Avro RJ85](/wiki/Avro_RJ85 "Avro RJ85"), registration CP-2933,[[1]](#cite_note-AH-1) [serial number](/wiki/Serial_number "Serial number") E.2348.[[2]](#cite_note-ASN-2) After service with other airlines and a period in storage between 2010 and 2013, it was acquired by [LaMia](/wiki/LaMia "LaMia"), a Venezuelan-owned airline operating out of Bolivia.[[3]](#cite_note-marca-3)

### Crew

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=LaMia_Flight_2933&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Crew")]

The captain was 36-year-old Miguel Alejandro Quiroga Murakami, who was a former [Bolivian Air Force](/wiki/Bolivian_Air_Force "Bolivian Air Force") (FAB) pilot and had previously flown for [EcoJet](/wiki/EcoJet_(airline) "EcoJet (airline)"), which also operated the Avro RJ85. He joined LaMia in 2013 and at the time of the accident he was one of the airline's co-owners as well as a flight instructor.[[4]](#cite_note-telegraph30nov2016-4) Quiroga had logged a total of 6,692 flight hours, including 3,417 hours on the Avro RJ85.[[5]](#cite_note-final_report-5): 20[[6]](#cite_note-6)

The first officer was 47-year-old Fernando Goytia, who was also a former FAB pilot. He received his type rating on the Avro RJ85 five months before the accident and had 6,923 flight hours, with 1,474 of them on the Avro RJ85.[[5]](#cite_note-final_report-5): 21[[7]](#cite_note-7)

29-year-old Sisy Arias, a trainee pilot, was an observer in the cockpit. She had been interviewed by TV before the flight.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## Accident

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=LaMia_Flight_2933&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Accident")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/LaMia_Flight_2933_route_map.svg/250px-LaMia_Flight_2933_route_map.svg.png)](/wiki/File:LaMia_Flight_2933_route_map.svg)

The planned (blue) and actual (red) itineraries from São Paulo to Medellín: The party flew with a different airline from São Paulo to Santa Cruz, where it boarded the LaMia aircraft. The refuelling stop at [Cobija](/wiki/Cobija "Cobija") was cancelled following a late departure from Santa Cruz.

The aircraft was carrying 73 passengers and four crew members[[10]](#cite_note-preliminaryreport-10): 3 on a flight from [Viru Viru International Airport](/wiki/Viru_Viru_International_Airport "Viru Viru International Airport"), in the Bolivian city of [Santa Cruz de la Sierra](/wiki/Santa_Cruz_de_la_Sierra "Santa Cruz de la Sierra"), to [José María Córdova International Airport](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Mar%C3%ADa_C%C3%B3rdova_International_Airport "José María Córdova International Airport"), serving [Medellín](/wiki/Medell%C3%ADn "Medellín") in Colombia, and located in nearby [Rionegro](/wiki/Rionegro "Rionegro").[[11]](#cite_note-CNN-11) Among the passengers were 22 players of the Brazilian [Associação Chapecoense de Futebol](/wiki/Associa%C3%A7%C3%A3o_Chapecoense_de_Futebol "Associação Chapecoense de Futebol") club, 23 staff, 21 journalists, and two guests.[[12]](#cite_note-12) The team was travelling to play their [away leg](/wiki/Two-legged_tie "Two-legged tie") of the [Final](/wiki/2016_Copa_Sudamericana_Finals "2016 Copa Sudamericana Finals") for the [2016 Copa Sudamericana](/wiki/2016_Copa_Sudamericana "2016 Copa Sudamericana") in Medellín against [Atlético Nacional](/wiki/Atl%C3%A9tico_Nacional "Atlético Nacional").[[13]](#cite_note-13)

### Background and transit to Bolivia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=LaMia_Flight_2933&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Background and transit to Bolivia")]

Chapecoense's initial request to [charter](/wiki/Air_charter "Air charter") LaMia for the whole journey from São Paulo to Medellín was refused by the [National Civil Aviation Agency of Brazil](/wiki/National_Civil_Aviation_Agency_of_Brazil "National Civil Aviation Agency of Brazil") because the limited scope of [freedom of the air](/wiki/Freedom_of_the_air "Freedom of the air") agreements between the two countries, under [International Civil Aviation Organization](/wiki/International_Civil_Aviation_Organization "International Civil Aviation Organization") rules, would have required the use of a Brazilian or Colombian airline for such a service.[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[5]](#cite_note-final_report-5): 7 The club opted to retain LaMia and arranged a flight with [Boliviana de Aviación](/wiki/Boliviana_de_Aviaci%C3%B3n "Boliviana de Aviación") from São Paulo to Santa Cruz de la Sierra, Bolivia, where it boarded the LaMia flight.[[17]](#cite_note-WSJ-17)[[18]](#cite_note-npr-18) LaMia had previously transported other teams for international competitions, including Chapecoense and the [Argentina national team](/wiki/Argentina_national_football_team "Argentina national football team"), which had flown on the same aircraft two weeks before.[[3]](#cite_note-marca-3)[[19]](#cite_note-19) The flight from São Paulo landed at Santa Cruz at 16:50 local time.[[20]](#cite_note-Consulado-de-Bolivia-20)

### Flight from Santa Cruz

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=LaMia_Flight_2933&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Flight from Santa Cruz")]

[![LaMia Flight 2933 is located in Colombia](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/Colombia_location_map.svg/330px-Colombia_location_map.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Colombia_location_map.svg "LaMia Flight 2933 is located in Colombia")

![LaMia Flight 2933](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Red_pog.svg/20px-Red_pog.svg.png)

Accident site within Colombia

The RJ85 operating LaMia flight 2933 departed Santa Cruz at 18:18 local time (22:18 UTC) on 28 November 2016.[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-Gulliver-22) A Chapecoense team member's request to have a video game retrieved from his luggage in the aircraft's cargo delayed departure.[[23]](#cite_note-23) The original flight plan included an intermediate [refueling stop](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/technical_stop "wiktionary:technical stop") at [the Cobija–Captain Aníbal Arab Airport](/wiki/Captain_An%C3%ADbal_Arab_Airport "Captain Aníbal Arab Airport"), near Bolivia's border with Brazil;[[22]](#cite_note-Gulliver-22)[[18]](#cite_note-npr-18) however, the flight's late departure meant the aircraft would not arrive at Cobija prior to the airport's closing time.[[2]](#cite_note-ASN-2) An officer of Bolivia's Airports and Air Navigation Services Administration ([AASANA](/wiki/AASANA "AASANA") – [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"): *Administración de Aeropuertos y Servicios Auxiliares a la Navegación Aérea*) at Santa Cruz de la Sierra reportedly rejected the crew's [flight plan](/wiki/Flight_plan "Flight plan") for a direct flight to Medellín several times despite pressure to approve it, because of the aircraft's range being almost the same as the flight distance. The flight plan was approved by another AASANA officer.[[24]](#cite_note-Bolivian_suspensions-24)[[a]](#cite_note-25) The distance between Santa Cruz and Medellín airports is 1,598 nautical miles (2,959 km; 1,839 mi).[[25]](#cite_note-gcm-26) A fuel stop in Cobija would have broken the flight into two segments, an initial segment of 514 nautical miles (952 km; 592 mi) to Cobija followed by a flight of 1,101 nautical miles (2,039 km; 1,267 mi) to Medellín, a total of 1,615 nautical miles (2,991 km; 1,859 mi).[[25]](#cite_note-gcm-26)

The flight crew anticipated a fuel consumption of 8,858 kg for their planned route of 1,611 nmi (including 200 kg for taxiing).[[5]](#cite_note-final_report-5): 57 After refueling at Santa Cruz, CP2933 had 9,073 kg on board.[[5]](#cite_note-final_report-5): 90 ICAO regulations would have required them to carry a total fuel load of 12,052 kg, to allow for holding, diversion, and other contingencies.[[5]](#cite_note-final_report-5): 71 The RJ85's fuel tanks have a capacity of 9,362 kg.[[5]](#cite_note-final_report-5): 70 Around 21:16 local time (02:16 UTC, 29 November), about 180 nmi from their destination, the aircraft displayed a low-fuel warning. At this point, they were 77 nmi from Bogotá, but the crew took no steps to divert there, nor to inform ATC of the situation.[[5]](#cite_note-final_report-5): 61 The RJ85 continued on course and began its descent towards Medellín at 21:30.[[26]](#cite_note-Jacdec-27)

Another aircraft had diverted to Medellín from its planned route (from Bogotá to San Andres) because of a suspected fuel leak.[[2]](#cite_note-ASN-2) Medellín [air traffic controllers](/wiki/Air_traffic_control "Air traffic control") gave that aircraft priority to land and at 21:43 the LaMia RJ85's crew was instructed to enter a [holding pattern](/wiki/Holding_(aeronautics) "Holding (aeronautics)") at the Rionegro [VOR](/wiki/VHF_omnidirectional_range "VHF omnidirectional range") beacon and wait with three other aircraft for its turn to land.[[26]](#cite_note-Jacdec-27)[[10]](#cite_note-preliminaryreport-10): 4–5 The crew requested and were given authorisation to hold at an [RNAV](/wiki/Area_navigation "Area navigation") [waypoint](/wiki/Waypoint "Waypoint") named GEMLI, about 5.4 nautical miles (10 km; 6 mi) south of the Rionegro VOR.[[10]](#cite_note-preliminaryreport-10): 5, 7 While waiting for the other aircraft to land, during the last 15 minutes of its flight, the RJ85 completed two laps of the holding pattern. This added approximately 54 nautical miles (100 km; 62 mi) to its flight path.[[27]](#cite_note-28)

At 21:49, the crew requested priority for landing because of unspecified "problems with fuel", and were told to expect an [approach](/wiki/Final_approach_(aeronautics) "Final approach (aeronautics)") clearance in "approximately seven minutes". At 21:52, a full 36 minutes after the onboard low-fuel warning had notified the crew of its situation, the crew finally declared a fuel emergency and requested immediate descent clearance and "vectors" for approach. At 21:53, with the aircraft nearing the end of its second lap of the holding pattern, engines 3 and 4 (the two engines on the right wing) [flamed out](/wiki/Flameout "Flameout") due to fuel exhaustion; engines 1 and 2 flamed out two minutes later, at which point the flight data recorder (FDR) stopped operating.[[2]](#cite_note-ASN-2)[[10]](#cite_note-preliminaryreport-10): 7–9 Shortly before 22:00, while flying in Colombian airspace between the municipalities of [La Ceja](/wiki/La_Ceja,_Antioquia "La Ceja, Antioquia") and [La Unión](/wiki/La_Uni%C3%B3n,_Antioquia "La Unión, Antioquia"), the pilot reported an electrical failure and fuel exhaustion.[[28]](#cite_note-29)[[29]](#cite_note-Lanacion-30) An air traffic controller radioed that the aircraft was 0.1 nautical miles (190 m; 200 yd) from the Rionegro VOR, but its altitude data were no longer being received.[[2]](#cite_note-ASN-2) The crew replied that the aircraft was at an altitude of 9,000 feet (2,700 m); the procedure for an aircraft approaching to land at José María Córdova International Airport states it must be at an altitude of at least 10,000 feet (3,000 m) when passing over the Rionegro VOR.[[2]](#cite_note-ASN-2) Air traffic control radar stopped detecting the aircraft at 21:55 local time as it descended among the mountains south of the airport.[[2]](#cite_note-ASN-2)[[26]](#cite_note-Jacdec-27)

At 21:59, the aircraft hit the crest of a ridge on a mountain known as Cerro Gordo at an altitude of 2,600 metres (8,500 ft) while flying in a northwesterly direction, with the wreckage of the rear of the aircraft on the southern side of the crest and other wreckage coming to rest on the northern side of the crest adjacent to the Rionegro VOR transmitter facility, which is in line with runway 01 at José María Córdova International Airport and about 18 kilometres (9.7 nmi; 11 mi) from the southern end.[[2]](#cite_note-ASN-2)[[26]](#cite_note-Jacdec-27)[[10]](#cite_note-preliminaryreport-10): 15

## Rescue

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=LaMia_Flight_2933&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Rescue")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Rescate_de_Polic%C3%ADa_al_vuelo_2933_-_01_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Rescate_de_Polic%C3%ADa_al_vuelo_2933_-_01_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rescate_de_Polic%C3%ADa_al_vuelo_2933_-_01_(cropped).jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Rescate_de_Polic%C3%ADa_al_vuelo_2933_-_06.jpg/250px-Rescate_de_Polic%C3%ADa_al_vuelo_2933_-_06.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rescate_de_Polic%C3%ADa_al_vuelo_2933_-_06.jpg)

Workers amidst the aircraft's wreckage on a Colombian mountainside

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/Rescate_de_Polic%C3%ADa_al_vuelo_2933_-_02.jpg/250px-Rescate_de_Polic%C3%ADa_al_vuelo_2933_-_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rescate_de_Polic%C3%ADa_al_vuelo_2933_-_02.jpg)

Recovery of bodies

Helicopters from the [Colombian Air Force](/wiki/Colombian_Aerospace_Force "Colombian Aerospace Force") were initially unable to get to the site because of heavy fog in the area,[[1]](#cite_note-AH-1) while first aid workers arrived two hours after the crash to find debris strewn across an area about 100 metres (330 ft) in diameter.[[30]](#cite_note-eltiempo-31) It was not until 02:00 on 29 November that the first survivor arrived at a hospital: [Alan Ruschel](/wiki/Alan_Ruschel "Alan Ruschel"), one of the Chapecoense team members.[[30]](#cite_note-eltiempo-31) Six people were found alive in the wreckage. The last survivor to be found was footballer [Neto](/wiki/Neto_(footballer,_born_1985) "Neto (footballer, born 1985)") who was discovered at 05:40.[[31]](#cite_note-caracol-32) Chapecoense backup goalkeeper [Jakson Follmann](/wiki/Jakson_Follmann "Jakson Follmann") underwent a potentially life-saving leg amputation.[[32]](#cite_note-33) 71 of the 77 occupants died as a result of the crash. The number of dead was initially thought to be 75, but it was later revealed that four people had not boarded the aircraft.[[33]](#cite_note-34) Colombian Air Force personnel extracted the bodies of 71 victims from the wreckage and took them to an air force base. They were then taken to the [Instituto de Medicina Legal](/wiki/Medical_jurisprudence "Medical jurisprudence") in Medellín for identification.[[34]](#cite_note-35)

## Investigation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=LaMia_Flight_2933&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Investigation")]

### Colombian crash investigation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=LaMia_Flight_2933&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Colombian crash investigation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/LaMia_Airlines_Flight_2933_Speed_and_Altitude.svg/250px-LaMia_Airlines_Flight_2933_Speed_and_Altitude.svg.png)](/wiki/File:LaMia_Airlines_Flight_2933_Speed_and_Altitude.svg)

Graphs of flight altitude and airspeed from Santa Cruz until the engine flameouts and consequent loss of electrical power stopped the FDR recording

The Air Accident Investigation Group (GRIAA – [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"): *Grupo de Investigación de Accidentes Aéreos*), the investigation group of Colombia's [Civil Aviation Authority](/wiki/Colombian_Civil_Aviation_Authority "Colombian Civil Aviation Authority") (UAEAC or Aerocivil – [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"): *Unidad Administrativa Especial de Aeronáutica Civil*),[[35]](#cite_note-36) investigated the accident, with assistance from [BAE Systems](/wiki/BAE_Systems "BAE Systems") (the successor company to [British Aerospace](/wiki/British_Aerospace "British Aerospace"), the aircraft’s manufacturer) and the British [Air Accidents Investigation Branch](/wiki/Air_Accidents_Investigation_Branch "Air Accidents Investigation Branch") (AAIB) as the investigative body of the state of the manufacturer. A team of three AAIB accident investigators was deployed.[[36]](#cite_note-AAIB-37) They were joined by investigators from Bolivia's national aviation authority, the General Directorate of Civil Aviation (DGAC – [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"): *[Dirección General de Aeronáutica Civil](/wiki/Directorate_General_of_Civil_Aeronautics_(Bolivia) "Directorate General of Civil Aeronautics (Bolivia)")*).[[37]](#cite_note-38) In all, twenty-three specialists were deployed on the investigation; in addition to ten Colombian investigators and those from Bolivia and the United Kingdom, Brazil and the United States contributed personnel to the investigation.[[38]](#cite_note-AerocivilPressRel-39) On the afternoon of 29 November the UAEAC reported that both [flight recorders](/wiki/Flight_recorder "Flight recorder") – the flight data recorder (FDR) and cockpit voice recorder (CVR) – had been recovered undamaged.[[1]](#cite_note-AH-1)

Evidence very quickly emerged to suggest that the aircraft had run out of fuel: the flight attendant who survived the accident reported that the captain's final words were "there is no fuel",[[39]](#cite_note-Metro6293649-40)[[40]](#cite_note-Sky10676541-41) and transmissions to that effect from the pilots to ATC were overheard by crews of other aircraft and recorded in the control tower.[[10]](#cite_note-preliminaryreport-10): 10[[41]](#cite_note-Reuters001-42) Shortly after the crash, the lead investigator stated that there was "no evidence of fuel in the aircraft" and the aircraft did not catch fire when it crashed.[[1]](#cite_note-AH-1) Analysis of the FDR showed all four engines flamed out a few minutes before the crash.[[10]](#cite_note-preliminaryreport-10): 9[[42]](#cite_note-43)[[43]](#cite_note-Mayday-44)

The investigation found that LaMia had consistently operated its fleet without the legally required endurance fuel load, and had simply been lucky to avoid any of the delays that the mandated fuel load were meant to allow for. An investigative report by Spanish-language American media company [Univision](/wiki/Univision "Univision"), using data from the [Flightradar24](/wiki/Flightradar24 "Flightradar24") website, claimed that the airline had broken the fuel and loading regulations of the [International Civil Aviation Organization](/wiki/International_Civil_Aviation_Organization "International Civil Aviation Organization") on 8 of its 23 previous flights since 22 August, three of which came within a month, including two direct flights from Medellín to Santa Cruz: one on 29 October transporting Chapecoense's final opponent, Atlético Nacional to the away leg of their Copa Sudamericana semifinal, and a flight without passengers on 4 November, and flights involving the [Argentina national team](/wiki/Argentina_national_football_team "Argentina national football team") to a match for the 2018 World Cup Qualifiers a week later. The report claimed the eight flights would have used at least some of the aircraft's mandatory fuel reserves (a variable fuel quantity to allow for an additional 45 minutes of flying time), concluding the company was accustomed to operating flights at the limit of the RJ85's endurance.[[44]](#cite_note-45)[[45]](#cite_note-46)[[46]](#cite_note-47)

### Findings in the final report

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=LaMia_Flight_2933&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Findings in the final report")]

On 27 April 2018, the investigators, led by Aerocivil, released the final investigative report for the crash of Flight 2933, listing the following causal factors:[[5]](#cite_note-final_report-5)

* The airline inappropriately planned the flight without considering the necessary amount of fuel that would be needed to fly to an alternate airport, fuel reserves, contingencies, or the required minimum fuel to land;
* The four engines shut down in sequence as a result of fuel exhaustion;
* Poor decision making by LaMia employees "as a result of processes that failed to ensure operational security";
* Poor decision making by the flight crew, who continued the flight on extremely limited fuel despite being aware of the low fuel levels aboard the aircraft and who did not take corrective actions to land the aircraft and refuel.

Additional contributing factors cited by the investigators were:

* Deploying the [landing gear](/wiki/Landing_gear "Landing gear") early;
* "Latent deficiencies" in the planning and execution of non-regular flights related to the insufficient supply of fuel;
* Specific deficiencies in the planning of the flight by LaMia;
* "Lack of supervision and operational control" by LaMia, which did not supervise the planning of the flight or its execution, nor did it provide advice to the flight crew;
* Failure to request priority or declare an emergency by the flight crew, particularly when fuel exhaustion became imminent; these actions would have allowed air traffic services to provide the necessary attention;
* Failure by the airline to follow the fuel management rules that the Bolivian DGAC had approved in certifying the company;
* CP-2933's late declaration of priority, and further time lost before declaration of fuel emergency, delayed critical time needed to reroute dense traffic already in approach vectors in the Ríonegro VOR area.

### Other findings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=LaMia_Flight_2933&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Other findings")]

The CVR had recorded the pilots discussing their fuel state and possible fuel stops *en route*,[[47]](#cite_note-48) but they were so accustomed to operating with minimal fuel that they decided against a fuel stop when ATC happened to assign them an adjustment in their route which saved a few minutes of flight time.[[43]](#cite_note-Mayday-44) For unknown reasons, the CVR stopped recording an hour and forty minutes before the FDR, when the aircraft was still about 550 nautical miles (1,020 km; 630 mi) away from the crash site at the Rionegro VOR.[[10]](#cite_note-preliminaryreport-10): 15 Aviation analyst John Nance and GRIAA investigators Julian Echeverri and Miguel Camacho would later suggest that the most probable explanation is that the flight's captain, who was also a part-owner of LaMia, pulled the circuit breaker on the CVR to prevent a record of the subsequent discussions, knowing that the flight did not have the appropriate fuel load.[[43]](#cite_note-Mayday-44)

The aircraft was estimated to be overloaded by nearly 400 kilograms (880 lb).[[48]](#cite_note-Sky10708788-49)

Due to restrictions imposed by the aircraft not being compliant with [reduced vertical separation minima](/wiki/Reduced_vertical_separation_minima "Reduced vertical separation minima") (RVSM) regulations, the submitted [flight plan](/wiki/Flight_plan "Flight plan"), with a nominated cruising [flight level](/wiki/Flight_level "Flight level") (FL) higher than 280 (approximately 28,000 feet (8,500 m) in altitude), was in violation of protocols. The flight plan, which was filed with AASANA, included a cruising altitude of FL300 (approximately 30,000 feet (9,100 m)).[[49]](#cite_note-Laprensa.com.bo-50)[[50]](#cite_note-bad-flight-plan-51) The flight plan was sent for review to Colombian and Brazilian authorities as well, in accordance with regional regulations.[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

### Bolivian criminal investigations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=LaMia_Flight_2933&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Bolivian criminal investigations")]

A week after the crash, [Bolivian police](/wiki/Law_enforcement_in_Bolivia "Law enforcement in Bolivia") detained the general director of LaMia, Gustavo Vargas Gamboa,[[24]](#cite_note-Bolivian_suspensions-24) on various charges including [involuntary manslaughter](/wiki/Involuntary_manslaughter "Involuntary manslaughter").[[51]](#cite_note-LAH_8-12-2016:_Bolivia_investigation-52) A warrant was issued for the arrest on the same charges of another of LaMia's co-owners, Marco Antonio Rocha Benegas, who fled to the United States.[[52]](#cite_note-eju-53)[[48]](#cite_note-Sky10708788-49) In 2017, Vargas Gamboa offered to plead guilty to negligent homicide if other charges were dropped;[[53]](#cite_note-54) prosecutors rejected this offer and in 2021 the case was filed in the Tenth Sentencing Court of the city of Santa Cruz, where it apparently continues[[52]](#cite_note-eju-53)[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*]

An arrest warrant was issued for Celia Castedo Monasterio,[[54]](#cite_note-mercopress-55) the employee of AASANA in Santa Cruz who had approved Flight 2933's flight plan - it was later approved by another official.[[24]](#cite_note-Bolivian_suspensions-24)[[51]](#cite_note-LAH_8-12-2016:_Bolivia_investigation-52) She fled the country seeking [political asylum](/wiki/Right_of_asylum "Right of asylum") in [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil"), claiming that after the crash she had been pressured by her superiors to alter a report she had made before the aircraft took off and that she feared that Bolivia would not give her a fair trial;[[55]](#cite_note-BBC_10122016:_Bolivia_detentions-56) she was placed under arrest in Brazil in 2024 pending extradition to Bolivia.[[54]](#cite_note-mercopress-55)

Gustavo Vargas Villegas, the son of Gustavo Vargas Gamboa, was detained for allegedly [using his influence](/wiki/Influence_peddling "Influence peddling") as director of DGAC's aircraft registry to have LaMia's aircraft given an operational clearance. A prosecutor involved with the case told reporters that "the prosecution has collected statements and evidence showing the participation of the accused in the crimes of misusing influence, conduct incompatible with public office and a breach of duties."[[55]](#cite_note-BBC_10122016:_Bolivia_detentions-56)[[24]](#cite_note-Bolivian_suspensions-24)

## Reactions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=LaMia_Flight_2933&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Reactions")]

### Governmental

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=LaMia_Flight_2933&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Governmental")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Chapecoense_-_Bandeiras_a_meio_mastro.jpg/250px-Chapecoense_-_Bandeiras_a_meio_mastro.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chapecoense_-_Bandeiras_a_meio_mastro.jpg)

Brazilian and [Mercosur](/wiki/Mercosur "Mercosur") flags at [half staff](/wiki/Half_staff "Half staff") at the National Congress Building in [Brasília](/wiki/Bras%C3%ADlia "Brasília")

Bolivia's General Directorate of Civil Aviation (DGAC – [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"): *[Dirección General de Aeronáutica Civil](/wiki/Direcci%C3%B3n_General_de_Aeron%C3%A1utica_Civil_(Bolivia) "Dirección General de Aeronáutica Civil (Bolivia)")*) suspended LaMia's [air operator's certificate](/wiki/Air_operator%27s_certificate "Air operator's certificate")[[24]](#cite_note-Bolivian_suspensions-24)[[56]](#cite_note-57) and impounded its remaining two RJ85s.[[51]](#cite_note-LAH_8-12-2016:_Bolivia_investigation-52) The Bolivian government suspended the director of the DGAC and the chief executive of AASANA as well as the director of the DGAC’s National Aeronautics Registry—the son of one of LaMia's owners.[[24]](#cite_note-Bolivian_suspensions-24)[[51]](#cite_note-LAH_8-12-2016:_Bolivia_investigation-52) Bolivia's Defense Minister expressed concern over the possibility of aviation sanctions and downgrades by foreign national aviation authorities, for which consequences may include banning Bolivian carriers from foreign airspace.[[57]](#cite_note-58)[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*]

Brazilian President [Michel Temer](/wiki/Michel_Temer "Michel Temer") declared three days of national mourning and requested that personnel from Brazil's embassy to Colombia in Bogotá be moved to Medellín to better assist the survivors and the families of the victims.[[58]](#cite_note-59)

### Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=LaMia_Flight_2933&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Sports")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Arena_Cond%C3%A1_tem_tributo_pelas_v%C3%ADtimas_de_voo_da_Chapecoense_02.jpg/250px-Arena_Cond%C3%A1_tem_tributo_pelas_v%C3%ADtimas_de_voo_da_Chapecoense_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Arena_Cond%C3%A1_tem_tributo_pelas_v%C3%ADtimas_de_voo_da_Chapecoense_02.jpg)

A vigil attended by thousands was held in [Chapecó](/wiki/Chapec%C3%B3 "Chapecó"), the hometown of Chapecoense

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/Minuto_de_Silencio_en_Pueblo_Nuevo.jpg/250px-Minuto_de_Silencio_en_Pueblo_Nuevo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Minuto_de_Silencio_en_Pueblo_Nuevo.jpg)

A [minute of silence](/wiki/Moment_of_silence "Moment of silence") observed before a football match in [San Cristóbal, Venezuela](/wiki/San_Crist%C3%B3bal,_T%C3%A1chira "San Cristóbal, Táchira")

Many South American football teams paid tribute to Chapecoense by changing their playing kits to include Chapecoense's badge or wearing Chapecoense's playing kit or green colours. Matches all over the world also began with a [minute of silence](/wiki/Moment_of_silence "Moment of silence").[[59]](#cite_note-60)[[60]](#cite_note-61)[[61]](#cite_note-62)[[62]](#cite_note-63)

#### CONMEBOL

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=LaMia_Flight_2933&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: CONMEBOL")]

All activities related to [CONMEBOL](/wiki/CONMEBOL "CONMEBOL") (the South American Football Confederation) were suspended immediately, including both legs of the [Copa Sudamericana final](/wiki/2016_Copa_Sudamericana_Finals "2016 Copa Sudamericana Finals"), scheduled for 30 November and 7 December,[[63]](#cite_note-crs-64) and the second leg of the [Copa do Brasil](/wiki/2016_Copa_do_Brasil_Finals "2016 Copa do Brasil Finals") Final.[[64]](#cite_note-65) Atlético Nacional, Chapecoense's opponents-to-be in the final, asked CONMEBOL to honor Chapecoense by awarding them the Copa Sudamericana title, stating that "for our part, and forever, Chapecoense are champions of the 2016 Copa Sudamericana".[[65]](#cite_note-66) CONMEBOL officially named Chapecoense the [2016 Copa Sudamericana](/wiki/2016_Copa_Sudamericana "2016 Copa Sudamericana") champions on 5 December.[[66]](#cite_note-67) The Brazilian team received the winner's prize money (US $2 million) and was awarded qualification to the [2017 Copa Libertadores](/wiki/2017_Copa_Libertadores "2017 Copa Libertadores"), [2017 Recopa Sudamericana](/wiki/2017_Recopa_Sudamericana "2017 Recopa Sudamericana") against [Atlético Nacional](/wiki/Atl%C3%A9tico_Nacional "Atlético Nacional") and the [2017 Suruga Bank Championship](/wiki/2017_Suruga_Bank_Championship "2017 Suruga Bank Championship") against [J1 League](/wiki/J1_League "J1 League") champions [Urawa Red Diamonds](/wiki/Urawa_Red_Diamonds "Urawa Red Diamonds").[[67]](#cite_note-68) Atlético Nacional also received the CONMEBOL Centennial Fair Play Award in recognition of its [sportsmanship](/wiki/Sportsmanship "Sportsmanship") in suggesting that Chapecoense be awarded the title.[[68]](#cite_note-69)

#### FIFA

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=LaMia_Flight_2933&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: FIFA")]

[FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA") president [Gianni Infantino](/wiki/Gianni_Infantino "Gianni Infantino") gave a speech at [Arena Condá](/wiki/Arena_Cond%C3%A1 "Arena Condá"), Chapecoense's stadium, at a public memorial. A committee representing FIFA at the service was composed of former football legends [Clarence Seedorf](/wiki/Clarence_Seedorf "Clarence Seedorf") and [Carles Puyol](/wiki/Carles_Puyol "Carles Puyol"); and [Real Madrid](/wiki/Real_Madrid_C.F. "Real Madrid C.F.") player [Lucas Silva](/wiki/Lucas_Silva_(footballer,_born_1993) "Lucas Silva (footballer, born 1993)"). Infantino gave his speech at the end of the service by saying: "Today we are all Brazilians, we are all Chapecoenses".[[69]](#cite_note-70) Nacional were awarded the [FIFA Fair Play Award](/wiki/FIFA_Fair_Play_Award "FIFA Fair Play Award") for requesting the Copa Sudamerica title to be awarded to Chapecoense.[[70]](#cite_note-71)

#### UEFA

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=LaMia_Flight_2933&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: UEFA")]

[UEFA](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA") officially asked for a minute's silence at all upcoming [Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League") and [Europa League](/wiki/Europa_League "Europa League") matches as a mark of respect.[[71]](#cite_note-72)[[72]](#cite_note-73)
President [Aleksander Ceferin](/wiki/Aleksander_Ceferin "Aleksander Ceferin") said in a statement: "European football is united in expressing its deepest sympathy to Chapecoense, the Brazilian football confederation, CONMEBOL and the families of all the victims following this week's air disaster".[[73]](#cite_note-74)

#### National football associations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=LaMia_Flight_2933&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: National football associations")]

The [Argentine Football Association](/wiki/Argentine_Football_Association "Argentine Football Association") sent a support letter to Chapecoense offering free loans of players from Argentine clubs.[[74]](#cite_note-75)

The [Brazilian Football Confederation](/wiki/Brazilian_Football_Confederation "Brazilian Football Confederation") (CBF) encouraged Chapecoense to play its next scheduled [Campeonato Brasileiro Série A](/wiki/Campeonato_Brasileiro_S%C3%A9rie_A "Campeonato Brasileiro Série A") game against [Clube Atlético Mineiro](/wiki/Clube_Atl%C3%A9tico_Mineiro "Clube Atlético Mineiro"), part of the final round of the tournament, as a tribute to the players.[[75]](#cite_note-76) Both Chapecoense and Atlético Mineiro refused to play the match, but they were not fined by the Superior Court of Sports Justice.[[76]](#cite_note-77)

Besides changing their profile pictures on social media to a black version of Chapecoense's badge and issuing messages of solidarity,[[77]](#cite_note-78) other Brazilian teams offered to loan the club players for the next year[[63]](#cite_note-crs-64) and asked the CBF to exempt Chapecoense from relegation for the next three years.[[78]](#cite_note-79)

In Colombia, a four-hour tribute took place at Atlético Nacional's stadium at the time Chapecoense's scheduled match would have kicked off. This was attended by 40,000 spectators with live coverage on [Fox Sports](/wiki/Fox_Sports "Fox Sports") and a live stream on YouTube.[[79]](#cite_note-80)[[80]](#cite_note-81)

The [Uruguayan Football Association](/wiki/Uruguayan_Football_Association "Uruguayan Football Association") declared two days of mourning.[[81]](#cite_note-82) The association's referees wore a Chapecoense badge on their shirts for the 14th matchday of the [Uruguayan Primera División](/wiki/Uruguayan_Primera_Divisi%C3%B3n "Uruguayan Primera División").[[82]](#cite_note-83)

### Other

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=LaMia_Flight_2933&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Other")]

[Avianca](/wiki/Avianca "Avianca"), Colombia's [flag carrier](/wiki/Flag_carrier "Flag carrier") and largest airline, provided 44 psychologists to help in the [counseling](/wiki/Grief_counseling "Grief counseling") of the families of the victims. The airline, by request of the Colombian and Brazilian governments, also provided logistical support and transportation to Brazilian medical personnel who were involved in the identification of the deceased.[[83]](#cite_note-84) On Twitter, Avianca expressed its regrets over the incident and stated that "our prayers are with the families of the victims".[[84]](#cite_note-85)

LaMia's insurance policy with Bolivian insurer Bisa had lapsed beginning in October 2016 for nonpayment; while said policy did not cover flights to Colombia, which the insurer included as part of a geographical exclusion clause along with several African countries, as well as Peru, Afghanistan, Syria and Iraq.[[85]](#cite_note-86) Nonetheless, the insurers agreed to fund a compensation scheme that would pay US$225,000 to each deceased passenger's family.[[86]](#cite_note-87)

During an interview, [Roberto Canessa](/wiki/Roberto_Canessa "Roberto Canessa"), a member of a Uruguayan rugby team that was travelling to a match in 1972 when their aircraft crashed in what became known as the [Andes flight disaster](/wiki/Uruguayan_Air_Force_Flight_571 "Uruguayan Air Force Flight 571"), said that he wanted to help the crash survivors.[[87]](#cite_note-88)[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*]

[Brazilian](/wiki/Brazilian_Football_Confederation "Brazilian Football Confederation") and [Colombian Football Federation](/wiki/Colombian_Football_Federation "Colombian Football Federation") arranged a friendly fundraiser game on 25 January 2017 in [Estádio Olímpico Nilton Santos](/wiki/Est%C3%A1dio_Ol%C3%ADmpico_Nilton_Santos "Estádio Olímpico Nilton Santos"), [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro"). Brazil beat Colombia 1-0 in a friendly that raised over £300,000 for the families of the Chapecoense players and staff who were killed in the plane crash.
[[88]](#cite_note-89)

Spanish club [FC Barcelona](/wiki/FC_Barcelona "FC Barcelona") hosted Chapecoense for a [friendly](/wiki/Exhibition_match "Exhibition match") [fundraiser](/wiki/Joan_Gamper_Trophy "Joan Gamper Trophy") on 7 August 2017, in order to help rebuild Chapecoense's team. Barcelona won 5–0. [Alan Ruschel](/wiki/Alan_Ruschel "Alan Ruschel"), one of the three surviving players, played his first game since the tragedy. He started the game as the captain and he played until the 35th minute, when he was substituted.[[89]](#cite_note-90)

In all copies of *[FIFA 17](/wiki/FIFA_17 "FIFA 17")*, players were given the Chapecoense emblem for free to wear for their FIFA Ultimate Team Club.[[90]](#cite_note-91)

## Survivors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=LaMia_Flight_2933&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Survivors")]

The surviving players were [Alan Ruschel](/wiki/Alan_Ruschel "Alan Ruschel"), [Jakson Follmann](/wiki/Jakson_Follmann "Jakson Follmann")[[91]](#cite_note-dpernambuco-92) and [Neto](/wiki/Neto_(footballer,_born_1985) "Neto (footballer, born 1985)").[[31]](#cite_note-caracol-32) The other survivors were a flight attendant and two other passengers.[[10]](#cite_note-preliminaryreport-10): 12 One of them, Erwin Tumiri, an employee of a Bolivian company contracted by LaMia to provide [maintenance technicians](/wiki/Aircraft_maintenance_engineer "Aircraft maintenance engineer") to accompany the aircraft, said that there was no announcement by the pilots that there was an emergency and he thought the aircraft was simply descending prior to the crash.[[92]](#cite_note-93) Tumiri would later be a survivor of the Colomi bus crash in 2021.[[93]](#cite_note-94)

Chapecoense goalkeeper [Danilo](/wiki/Danilo_(footballer,_born_1985) "Danilo (footballer, born 1985)") was initially reported to have survived the crash and to have been taken to a hospital, where he later succumbed to his injuries.[[94]](#cite_note-BBC38140929-95) However, the San Vicente Fundación hospital from Medellín clarified a few days later that he died in the crash.[[95]](#cite_note-96)

Brazilian radio personality [Rafael Henzel](/wiki/Rafael_Henzel "Rafael Henzel"), who was a passenger on the flight and the only journalist to survive, later died on 26 March 2019 from a [heart attack](/wiki/Myocardial_infarction "Myocardial infarction").[[96]](#cite_note-97)

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=LaMia_Flight_2933&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

The United States cable TV network [ESPN](/wiki/ESPN "ESPN") produced an hour long story about the crash for its [E:60](/wiki/E:60 "E:60") news magazine TV show. The episode focused on how one of the pilots was also a co-owner of the airline company and the effects on the survivors and on family members of the people killed in the accident.[[102]](#cite_note-103)

The crash of LaMia Flight 2933 was covered in ["Football Tragedy"](/wiki/List_of_Mayday_episodes#ep169 "List of Mayday episodes"), a Season 19 (2019) episode of the internationally syndicated Canadian TV documentary series *[Mayday](/wiki/Mayday_(Canadian_TV_series) "Mayday (Canadian TV series)")* (*Air Crash Investigation* for outside Canada and the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States")).[[43]](#cite_note-Mayday-44)

The [Rooster Teeth](/wiki/Rooster_Teeth "Rooster Teeth") podcast *Black Box Down* reviewed this incident in Episode 109.[[103]](#cite_note-BlackBoxDown-104)
